2FA.description: A sign-in method that requires two forms of verification.
2FA.example: Enable 2FA before joining the production GitHub organization.

ACK.description: Used to confirm that a message, request, or update has been received.
ACK.example: ACK, I will review the PR after standup.

API.description: A contract that lets software systems communicate with each other.
API.example: The mobile app calls the payments API during checkout.

ASAP.description: Used when something should be handled quickly.
ASAP.example: Please share the incident timeline ASAP.

B2B.description: A product, service, or process intended for business customers.
B2B.example: This onboarding flow is for our B2B customers.

B2C.description: A product, service, or process intended for individual consumers.
B2C.example: The B2C signup funnel needs fewer required fields.

CD.description: A software delivery practice where changes are kept ready to release.
CD.example: The CD pipeline packages every successful main branch build.

CDN.description: A distributed network that serves content from locations closer to users.
CDN.example: Static assets are served through the CDN.

CI.description: A development practice where automated builds and tests run after code changes.
CI.example: CI failed because one unit test is flaky.

CI/CD.description: A combined workflow for automatically testing, building, and preparing software for release.
CI/CD.example: The CI/CD workflow runs tests before deployment.

CLI.description: A text-based way to interact with a program from a terminal.
CLI.example: Use the CLI to install the FullForm Quick Action.

CRUD.description: The four common operations for managing stored data.
CRUD.example: The admin page needs CRUD support for glossary entries.

CSS.description: A language used to style web pages.
CSS.example: The button spacing is controlled in CSS.

DB.description: A system or store used to persist structured data.
DB.example: The user profile is saved in the DB.

DNS.description: The internet system that maps domain names to network addresses.
DNS.example: Check DNS if the new domain is not resolving.

E2E.description: Testing or behavior that covers a complete user flow from start to finish.
E2E.example: The E2E test covers login, checkout, and receipt generation.

EOD.description: Usually means before the end of the current workday.
EOD.example: Please send the deployment status by EOD.

ETA.description: An estimate for when something will be ready or completed.
ETA.example: What is the ETA for the bug fix?

FYI.description: Used to share information that may not require action.
FYI.example: FYI, the release branch was cut this morning.

HTML.description: The markup language used to structure web pages.
HTML.example: The email template uses basic HTML.

HTTP.description: A protocol used for transferring resources on the web.
HTTP.example: The API returned an HTTP 404 response.

IP.description: A network protocol used to address and route data between devices.
IP.example: Allowlist the office IP before testing the staging service.

IRL.description: Used to distinguish offline or in-person context from online discussion.

JSON.description: A lightweight text format for structured data exchange.
JSON.example: The glossary is stored as JSON.

JWT.description: A compact token format commonly used to pass identity or authorization claims.
JWT.example: The API validates the JWT before returning user data.

KPI.description: A metric used to measure progress toward a business or product goal.
KPI.example: Activation rate is the KPI for this onboarding project.

LGTM.description: Used in code review or chat to indicate approval.

MFA.description: Authentication that requires multiple verification factors.
MFA.example: MFA is required for admin accounts.

MR.description: A request to merge code changes, commonly used in GitLab workflows.
MR.example: Please add screenshots to the MR description.

MVP.description: The smallest useful version of a product that can validate an idea.
MVP.example: The MVP supports exact acronym lookup only.

NACK.description: Used to reject or disagree with a request, proposal, or message.
NACK.example: NACK on releasing today until the rollback plan is ready.

OKR.description: A goal-setting framework with qualitative objectives and measurable results.
OKR.example: The team OKR is to reduce onboarding time.

OOO.description: Used when someone is unavailable because they are away from work.

ORM.description: A technique or library that maps application objects to database tables.
ORM.example: The ORM migration adds a new users table.

POC.description: A small implementation used to prove an idea is technically possible.
POC.example: Build a POC before committing to the integration.

PR.description: A request to review and merge code changes into another branch.
PR.example: Open a PR once the tests pass.

PRD.description: A document that describes product goals, requirements, and expected behavior.
PRD.example: The PRD defines the first version of the search experience.

PTO.example: I am taking PTO next Monday.

QA.description: The process of checking that software meets quality expectations.
QA.example: QA found a regression in the checkout flow.

RCA.description: An investigation into the underlying cause of an incident or problem.
RCA.example: We will share the RCA after the incident review.

REST.description: An architectural style often used for web APIs.
REST.example: The service exposes a REST API for account lookup.

RFC.description: A document shared to gather feedback on a proposal or design.
RFC.example: Please review the RFC before Friday.

RPC.description: A pattern where one program calls a procedure on another system.
RPC.example: The frontend talks to the backend through RPC calls.

SDK.description: A collection of tools, libraries, or documentation for building with a platform.
SDK.example: Install the iOS SDK before building the sample app.

SLA.description: A formal commitment about expected service performance or support.
SLA.example: The enterprise SLA requires a response within one hour.

SLI.description: A metric used to measure service reliability or performance.
SLI.example: Request latency is one of our SLIs.

SLO.description: A target value for a service reliability or performance metric.
SLO.example: The API has a 99.9% availability SLO.

SQL.description: A language used to query and manage relational databases.
SQL.example: Use SQL to inspect the failed import records.

SSO.description: An authentication setup where one login gives access to multiple systems.

TBD.description: Used when a decision has not been made yet.

TLS.description: A protocol used to encrypt network communication.
TLS.example: The service requires TLS for all external traffic.

TL;DR.description: A short summary of a longer message or document.
TL;DR.example: Add a TL;DR at the top of the design doc.

TLDR.description: A short summary of a longer message or document.
TLDR.example: Add a TLDR at the top of the design doc.

UI.description: The part of a product that users see and interact with.
UI.example: The UI should show a clear empty state.

URI.description: A string that identifies a resource by name, location, or both.
URI.example: The callback URI must match the OAuth app settings.

URL.description: A web address that identifies where a resource is located.
URL.example: Share the staging URL in the release channel.

UX.description: The overall experience a person has while using a product.
UX.example: The UX should make the next action obvious.

VPN.description: A secure network connection used to access private systems.
VPN.example: Connect to the VPN before accessing staging logs.

WIP.description: Used when something is not finished yet.
WIP.example: Mark the PR as WIP until the migration is ready.

XML.description: A markup language for structured documents and data.
XML.example: The legacy integration still sends XML payloads.

YAML.description: A human-readable data serialization format often used for configuration.
YAML.example: The CI workflow is configured in YAML.
